episode_title: Pediatricians: Not About The Benjamins, Baby
host: Dr. Jessica Steier & Dr. Sarah Scheinman
summary: This episode examines the financial relationship between pediatricians and vaccine administration, debunking the claim that pediatricians receive significant financial incentives to vaccinate children. The hosts present findings from a multi-month research report showing that vaccines are actually the second-highest cost in pediatric practices (after staff salaries), with reimbursement barely covering costs. Three practicing pediatricians share real-world experiences demonstrating the financial burden and regulatory complexity of vaccine delivery, while also discussing vaccine hesitancy trends and the importance of shared clinical decision-making in patient care.
science_topics: Meningitis caused by Haemophilus influenzae type b and Streptococcus pneumoniae
MMR vaccine and congenital rubella syndrome
Vaccine storage, handling, and cold chain management
Immunization policy and vaccine delivery systems
claims_to_note: Claim: Pediatricians receive large financial bonuses from pharmaceutical companies for vaccinating children - DEBUNKED by hosts as illegal and not occurring

Claim: Vaccine reimbursement from insurance covers or exceeds the cost of vaccines - CONTRADICTED; hosts state reimbursement 'pretty much' covers cost or is slightly above, but does not account for storage, administration, or waste
Claim: One in four pediatricians have considered stopping vaccine provision due to financial viability - requires verification against NASEM data
Claim: Pneumococcal meningitis causes are now rare due to vaccination - stated as accurate by Dr. Goza based on clinical experience
Claim: Rubella exposure during pregnancy poses significant risk of congenital defects - accurate, supported by historical and current medical knowledge
policy_relevance: CDC vaccine recommendations and January 2026 policy changes regarding shared clinical decision-making
Vaccines for Children (VFC) program - regulatory requirements and reimbursement limitations
Quality incentive programs from insurance companies tied to immunization rates
American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) leadership involvement in vaccine policy
State-by-state variations in vaccine economics and regulatory requirements
Pharmaceutical manufacturer payment regulations (Physician Payments Sunshine Act implications)
key_quotes: Pediatricians go to extreme lengths to make sure that their patients can get vaccines to keep them healthy... if anything, the narrative is not true, and pediatricians are taking a loss and aren't able to sustain their practice while giving vaccines.

My job as a pediatrician is to encourage families to do the things that are going to keep their kids healthy... it does mean that every time you come in, we're going to talk about what the risks and the benefits are of the vaccine because that's my job.

We do not go into pediatrics to make large profits. We go into pediatrics because we care about kids... our entire career choices have gone down a path knowing we are going to make less and still work really hard to take care of kids because we want to see kids healthy and thriving.

Effects of Human-Caused Greenhouse Gas Emissions on U.S. Climate, Health, and Welfare
publications.description: The scientific community has been studying the question of how human-caused emissions of greenhouse gases are affecting the climate for well over a century. Much is known today, drawing on decades of direct observations of the Earth system and detailed research. This report summarizes the latest evidence on whether greenhouse gas emissions threaten public health and welfare in the United States. The impetus for this report was a notice of proposed rulemaking issued in August 2025 by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) indicating its intention to rescind the 2009 Finding of Endangerment and Cause or Contribute Findings for Greenhouse Gases Under Section 202(a) of the Clean Air Act. Recognizing that significantly more evidence is available today, the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine launched this study to review newly available scientific evidence on the topics included in a Technical Support Document that EPA prepared to inform its decision-making on

publications.title: Incorporating Resilience into Transportation Networks
publications.description: Transportation agencies have been presented with increased disruptions to supply chains and other transportation networks caused by naturally occurring and other unanticipated events, such as extreme weather events and the COVID-19 pandemic. These disruptions can substantially affect the resilience of the supply chain against unanticipated disruptions and ultimately affect the economic vitality of the industries and communities involved. NCHRP Research Report 1118: Incorporating Resilience into Transportation Networks, from TRB's National Cooperative Highway Research Program, provides a guide for state departments of transportation and other transportation agencies seeking to define and strategically integrate resilience into transportation networks and network planning.

publications.title: Countering the Pandemic Threat Through Global Coordination on Vaccines: The Influenza Imperative
publications.description: The COVID-19 pandemic has laid bare the fragility of the global system of preparedness and response to pandemics and the fragmentation of our research and development ecosystem. The pandemic has provided a disruptive moment to advance new norms and frameworks for influenza. It also has demonstrated how innovative global public-private partnerships and coordination mechanisms can lead to rapid successes in viral vaccine research, manufacturing, and risk pooling. Countering the Pandemic Threat Through Global Coordination on Vaccines identifies ways to strengthen pandemic and seasonal influenza global coordination, partnerships, and financing. This report presents seven overarching recommendations for how the urgent influenza threat should be conceptualized and prioritized within the global pandemic preparedness and response agenda in the future.

publications.title: Adverse Effects of Vaccines: Evidence and Causality
publications.description: In 1900, for every 1,000 babies born in the United States, 100 would die before their first birthday, often due to infectious diseases. Today, vaccines exist for many viral and bacterial diseases. The National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act, passed in 1986, was intended to bolster vaccine research and development through the federal coordination of vaccine initiatives and to provide relief to vaccine manufacturers facing financial burdens. The legislation also intended to address concerns about the safety of vaccines by instituting a compensation program, setting up a passive surveillance system for vaccine adverse events, and by providing information to consumers. A key component of the legislation required the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to collaborate with the Institute of Medicine to assess concerns about the safety of vaccines and potential adverse events, especially in children. publications.committee: Board on Population Health and Public Health Practice

publications.title: Immunization Safety Review: Multiple Immunizations and Immune Dysfunction
Immunization Safety Review: Measles-Mumps-Rubella Vaccine and Autism
publications.description: This eighth and final report of the Immunization Safety Review Committee examines the hypothesis that vaccines, specifically the measles-mumps-rubella (MMR) vaccine and thimerosal-containing vaccines, are causally associated with autism. The committee reviewed the extant published and unpublished epidemiological studies regarding causality and studies of potential biologic mechanisms by which these immunizations might cause autism. Immunization Safety Review: Vaccines and Autism finds that the body of epidemiological evidence favors rejection of a causal relationship between thimerosal-containing vaccines and autism. The book further finds that potential biological mechanisms for vaccine-induced autism that have been generated to date are only theoretical. It recommends a public health response that fully supports an array of vaccine safety activities and recommends that available funding for autism research be channeled to the most promising areas. publications.committee: Board on Health Promotion and Disease Prevention

Knowledge Management at State Departments of Transportation: Research Roadmap
publications.description: "In most state departments of transportation (DOTs), a significant portion of the workforce continues to be eligible for retirement, which could lead to a loss of institutional knowledge. Collaboration and interdisciplinary work are also increasing the need for effective, efficient, and timely knowledge transfer. State DOTs and other transportation agencies could benefit from knowledge management (KM) techniques and practices to help identify, capture, and transfer institutional knowledge and support continuous learning. KM continues to evolve, particularly in the understanding of factors such as cognitive science, learning cultures, and knowledge risk. NCHRP Research Report 1134: Knowledge Management at State Departments of Transportation: Research Roadmap, from TRB's National Cooperative Highway Research Program, presents a research roadmap for promoting KM among state DOTs and other transportation agencies.
